metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate BOTH YAML arms (agentic verify_branch then-arm ~line 174 and non-agentic else-arm ~line 252) as independent edit targets for all three YAML changes (coach_inputs.with.blocking, coach_gate.when, coach_notes.surviving).
evidence: YAML line 174: first coach_inputs block (verify_branch agentic then-arm)
YAML line 252: second coach_inputs block (verify_branch non-agentic else-arm)
Plan says: 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: … to the coach_inputs step's with:; change coach_gate's when: …; change coach_notes's surviving: …' — described once, no mention of two identical arm pairs
findings.finding: plan-review.yaml has TWO structurally identical coach_inputs/coach_gate blocks (agentic arm: lines 174-226; non-agentic arm: lines 252-304), but the plan describes the YAML edits once without acknowledging both arms as independent edit sites. An implementer applying the change to only one arm would leave the other arm un-widened.
findings.location: plan-review.yaml (What section, YAML edit description); lines 174-226 and 252-304
scenarios: Implementer applies YAML changes to the agentic arm only; non-agentic arm (single-turn verifier path) still gates on surfaced-only and passes empty surviving to coach_notes for blocking-only verdicts.
CI passes on plans that trigger agentic verify but fails silently for plans that take the non-agentic path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: …' with 'plan-review.yaml: apply the following three changes identically to BOTH coach_inputs/coach_gate blocks (agentic arm, ~line 174; non-agentic arm, ~line 252): …'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the call-chain description: `pass4_coach` calls `review_kernel.coach()` which internally calls `render_coach_notes`; explicitly state whether `coach()` signature also needs updating to thread the decision map, or confirm decision tag is attached post-render.
evidence: passes.py:871: `return review_kernel.coach(surviving, move_registry, pick=_pick, active_triggers=triggers)`
coach.py:217: `return render_coach_notes(raw, applicable)` — the internal call inside `coach()`
Plan: 'both callers (the bespoke pass4 coach in passes.py and plan_review_coach) pass it' — misidentifies the call chain
findings.finding: The plan states 'both callers (the bespoke pass4_coach in passes.py and plan_review_coach) pass [the decision map to render_coach_notes]', but passes.py's `pass4_coach` (line 842) does NOT call `render_coach_notes` directly — it calls `review_kernel.coach()` (line 871), which internally calls `render_coach_notes` at coach.py:217. The internal `review_kernel.coach()` → `render_coach_notes` call is an unenumerated consumer if render_coach_notes gains a decision map parameter, and the plan's description of how `pass4_coach` 'passes it' is inaccurate.
findings.location: What section (render_coach_notes decision map); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:871 and src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:217
scenarios: Implementer adds decision map parameter to render_coach_notes as keyword-only but forgets to thread it through coach() → render_coach_notes; the bespoke test path (pass4_coach) never stamps decision on rendered notes.
AC1 unit test asserting decision='block' on a bespoke-path note fails because the decision map never reached render_coach_notes via the coach() internal call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly note in the What section that all YAML `coach_inputs`/`coach_gate` mutations must be applied to BOTH the `verify_branch` then-arm (code-grounded) and else-arm (non-code-grounded) copies.
evidence: YAML lines 174-226: first `coach_inputs` + `coach_gate` block (code-grounded arm).
YAML lines 252-304: second identical `coach_inputs` + `coach_gate` block (non-code-grounded arm).
Plan says: 'plan-review.yaml: add `blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }}` to the `coach_inputs` step's `with:`' — no mention that this must be done in BOTH locations.
findings.finding: The YAML `plan-review.yaml` contains two fully duplicated copies of the `coach_inputs` + `coach_gate` blocks — one inside the `verify_branch` `then:` arm (code-grounded path, lines 174-226) and one inside the `else:` arm (non-code-grounded path, lines 252-304). The plan's 'What' section describes the YAML changes generically without acknowledging the duplication, so an implementor who updates only one copy silently leaves the other arm unchanged — a structural incompleteness.
findings.location: What section: 'plan-review.yaml: add `blocking:` to coach_inputs, change coach_gate when:, change coach_notes surviving:'; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 174-226 and 252-304
scenarios: Implementor updates the first `coach_inputs` block (code-grounded arm) and forgets the second; blocking-finding coaching works only when code-grounded criteria are present.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers (the bespoke pass4 coach in passes.py and `plan_review_coach`) pass it, so every coaching entry carries `decision`.' But `passes.render_coach_notes` is a thin alias of `_coach.render_coach_notes` (review_kernel/coach.py:162) — a simple `(raw_notes, registry) -> list` function with no decision-map parameter. The plan never lists `review_kernel/coach.py render_coach_notes` as a change site, so the kernel function must be modified but is absent from the What section.
findings.location: What section / passes.py:75, review_kernel/coach.py:162-185

findings.finding: The plan-review.yaml workflow contains TWO structurally-identical coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes blocks — one inside the `code_grounded` then-arm (lines ~174-226) and an identical copy inside the else-arm (lines ~252-304). The plan's three YAML changes (add `blocking:` to coach_inputs, change coach_gate `when:`, change coach_notes `surviving:`) must be applied to BOTH arms, but the plan only describes them once without acknowledging the duplication.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml, lines 174-226 (then-arm) and 252-304 (else-arm); plan section 'What' / 'plan-review.yaml' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'What / plan-review.yaml' bullet: 'Apply all three changes in BOTH arms of verify_branch (the code_grounded then-arm and else-arm are structurally identical; each contains its own coach_inputs / coach_gate / coach_notes block).'

findings.finding: The workflow YAML (plan-review.yaml) has TWO duplicate structural arms — the `code_grounded` agentic arm (then:) and the single-turn arm (else:) — each containing their own `coach_inputs`, `coach_gate`, and `coach_notes` steps. The plan's YAML change instructions name the step ids only once without calling out that both arms must be updated, creating a risk that only one arm receives the widening.
findings.location: plan-review.yaml: lines 174-211 (agentic arm) and lines 252-304 (single-turn arm); plan 'What' section: 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: … to the coach_inputs step's with:; change coach_gate's when:; change coach_notes's surviving: binding'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Note: plan-review.yaml has two identical coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes sequences (one inside verify_branch.then, one inside verify_branch.else); all changes must be applied to both arms.'

findings.finding: plan-review.yaml duplicates the coach_inputs + coach_gate steps verbatim in BOTH arms of the verify_branch (agentic arm lines 174-226, non-agentic arm lines 253-304). The plan says 'update the coach_gate branch when: condition' (singular) and 'widen the coach_inputs binding', but neither the What section nor the YAML edit description mentions that there are TWO copies to update. An implementer updating only the first (agentic) arm leaves the non-agentic arm with the old advisory-only behavior.
findings.location: What section (workflow path bullet) / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 174-226 and 253-304

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact signature change and return-value augmentation for `render_coach_notes` (or its call wrapper) that makes `decision` appear in each emitted coaching entry, including the lookup key (finding_ref id → decision tag) so both callers can construct the map unambiguously.
evidence: Code-confirmed: `render_coach_notes` returns `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching}` — no `decision` key.

Plan: 'passes.render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py) gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers (the bespoke pass4_coach in passes.py and `plan_review_coach`) pass it, so every coaching entry carries `decision`'
Sidecar slim plan says: 'add `decision` to that list so the tag survives persistence' — but if the renderer doesn't emit it, `c.get('decision')` always yields None.
findings.finding: `render_coach_notes` (the kernel single source re-exported as `passes.render_coach_notes`) currently returns coaching entries with a fixed key set `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching}` and no `decision` field. The plan says this function 'gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory")' and that 'both callers pass it', but never specifies how the function signature changes or how `decision` flows into each returned entry — leaving the mechanism by which `decision` appears in the rendered output structurally undefined.
findings.location: What section: 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map'; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162-185
scenarios: An implementor updates both call sites to pass the decision map but never adds the map-lookup logic inside `render_coach_notes`; the `decision` field is silently absent from every coaching entry; the sidecar and AC-1 oracle both fail.

An implementor adds `decision` inside `render_coach_notes` but interprets the plan as requiring a per-entry lookup from `finding_refs` to the map — the exact lookup key (finding id string vs index) is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`render_coach_notes` gains an optional `decision_map: dict[str, str] | None = None` parameter; when supplied, each emitted entry sets `decision = decision_map.get(fid)` for the first `fid` in `finding_refs` (or the first matching fid); callers build the map as `{f["id"]: ("block" if f in blocking_set else "advisory") for f in coachable}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the What section that `workflow_ops.py` line 497 (`surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or [])`) must be replaced with a union of both `blocking` and `surfaced` when constructing the trigger set passed to `applicable_moves`.
evidence: Code: `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or [])` followed by `triggers = {c for f in surviving for c in f.get("criteria", []) or []}` — blocking criteria are excluded.
Plan: 'derive the trigger set from the union of `blocking` + `surfaced` (it already receives both), matching coach_inputs' — without naming the variable to replace or showing the union construction.
findings.finding: `plan_review_coach` op builds its applicable-moves trigger set from `surfaced` (advisory) findings only — code-confirmed at `workflow_ops.py:497-499`. The plan says the op should 'derive the trigger set from the union of `blocking` + `surfaced`', but this is described only in a one-liner without specifying which variable (`surviving` at line 497) must be replaced, leaving the exact line-level change ambiguous and easy to miss given the variable is aliased to `surfaced` three lines above where the comment about 'matching coach_inputs' already exists.
findings.location: What section: 'plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475): derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:497-499
scenarios: An implementor reads 'matching coach_inputs' and updates `coach_inputs` correctly but leaves `plan_review_coach`'s trigger-set line unchanged because the variable name `surviving` looks right contextually.

A blocking finding carrying a criterion that no advisory finding carries is silently excluded from the trigger set, so a move that `applies_when` that criterion is filtered out and no coaching is produced for that blocking finding.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'In `plan_review_coach`, replace the `surviving` trigger-set line (line 497) with `all_coachable = list(ctx.inputs.get("blocking") or []) + list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or []); triggers = {c for f in all_coachable for c in f.get("criteria", []) or []}`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC3 grep oracle to also cover `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py` (e.g. add `&& grep -niP "advisory\\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py` returning no hits), OR fold the coach.py header check into the unit test assertion so no part of AC3 relies solely on the single-file grep.
evidence: AC3 grep: `grep -niP "advisory\\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md`
What section: 'the hardcoded listing header `## Surviving advisory findings (by id)` in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 becomes decision-neutral'
Grep does not include src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py in its target path
findings.finding: AC3's grep oracle (`grep -niP "advisory\\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md`) targets only the .md file, but the plan requires a second change in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123` (header from '## Surviving advisory findings (by id)' → '## Surviving findings (by id)'). An executor who updates the .md but forgets coach.py would see the grep pass while the coach.py header still contains 'advisory findings' — making the grep oracle incomplete for the full AC3 claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 oracle: `grep -niP ...plan_review_coach.md`
scenarios: Executor updates plan_review_coach.md but misses coach.py:123; grep passes, AC3 is falsely satisfied, and the listing header remains advisory-framed in production output.
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the AC3 oracle to:
```
grep -niP "advisory\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py
```
returning no hits, AND the existing unit test assertion on the rendered listing header.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit oracle to AC2 (or a new AC) that asserts `guide_url` is present on the blocking finding's coaching entry when exercised through the workflow/orchestrator path (`plan_review_coach` op + `orchestrator.py:515-530`), not solely through the test-only `pass4_coach` path.
evidence: 'The live production path is EXCLUSIVELY the workflow ops above - pass4_coach in passes.py (:842) is exercised only by unit tests'
AC1 oracle: 'python -m pytest tests/unit -k "coach" -x' — targets passes.py test path
AC2 oracle: '...asserting a note referencing the blocking finding renders with decision="block"' — no guide_url assertion for the workflow path
'guide_url: … verify by test' — no named AC or test location specified for the orchestrator passthrough
findings.finding: The guide_url attachment via the production path (orchestrator.py:515-530) is not explicitly exercised for blocking findings in any AC. AC1 tests the test-only `pass4_coach` path (passes.py) which the plan explicitly notes is 'exercised only by unit tests'; AC2's workflow oracle only asserts decision='block' is present, not guide_url. The plan asserts guide_url is 'for free' and says 'verify by test' without naming which AC covers the production orchestrator path — leaving the live guide_url attachment for blocking findings potentially untested.
findings.location: AC1 oracle / AC2 oracle / What section: 'guide_url: the passthrough attach at orchestrator.py:515-530 … verify by test'
scenarios: Agent writes AC1 test over passes.py, AC2 test over workflow ops (without guide_url assertion), both pass, but orchestrator.py:515-530 never attaches guide_url to blocking entries in production because it keys off a field that blocking entries lacked before this change.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's `plan_review_coach` workflow test oracle to also assert `guide_url is not None` on the rendered coaching entry for the blocking finding, confirming the orchestrator passthrough works in the production code path.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `grep -niP "advisory\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the prompt's YAML frontmatter `description:` field (and optionally `title:`) as explicit rewrite targets in the What section alongside the body reframing, so the decision-neutral update is complete.
evidence: Prompt frontmatter line 4: `description: Pass 4 of the plan-review gate (advisory; after the gate decision) — maps each surviving advisory finding to a move`
Prompt frontmatter line 3: `title: Plan-review Pass-4 affirmative coach` — not advisory-tagged but the description is.
Prompt body line 13: 'For each SURVIVING advisory finding' — caught by the AC-3 grep.
Plan says to reframe the prompt as decision-neutral but only names the body text implicitly; the frontmatter fields are not enumerated.
findings.finding: The prompt `plan_review_coach.md` contains 'SURVIVING advisory finding' in its body (line 13) and the YAML frontmatter `description:` field contains 'maps each surviving advisory finding'. The plan's AC-3 oracle grep `grep -niP "advisory\s+(findings|only)"` catches 'advisory finding' (singular, line 13) and the frontmatter description. However the plan's 'What' section only says 'reframed decision-neutral' and points to the body text — it does not enumerate that the prompt's YAML frontmatter `title:` ('advisory; after the gate decision') and `description:` ('maps each surviving advisory finding') fields also require rewriting. This is an instruction-locality gap: the rewrite target is incompletely specified.
findings.location: What section: 'plan_review_coach.md reframed decision-neutral'; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md lines 1-18
scenarios: Implementor rewrites the body text (passing the AC-3 grep) but leaves the YAML frontmatter description unchanged; the metadata still frames the prompt as advisory-only, which may cause confusion or incorrect tooling behavior if the description is used downstream.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that `render_coach_notes` and `coach_listing` live in the shared `review_kernel.coach` module (not plan-review-owned), enumerate the code-review call site at `code_review/workflow_ops.py:501`, and confirm that any signature change uses an optional-with-default parameter so the code-review gate is unaffected.
evidence: Plan: 'passes.render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py) gains an optional decision map'
passes.py:75: `render_coach_notes = _coach.render_coach_notes` — thin alias, not plan-review-owned code
review_kernel/coach.py:162: `def render_coach_notes(raw_notes, registry)` — the actual function
code_review/workflow_ops.py:501: `coaching = review_kernel.render_coach_notes(raw_notes, applicable)` — second consumer not mentioned in the plan
Plan Scope section: 'OUT: any change to move-registry contents, the subject validator...' — does not exclude shared kernel changes but also does not acknowledge them
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory")' and frames this as a plan-review change, but `passes.render_coach_notes` is a thin module-level alias (`_coach.render_coach_notes`) pointing to `review_kernel.coach.render_coach_notes` — a shared kernel function that is also called by `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501`. Any signature change (adding a `decision` parameter) to this kernel function touches the code-review gate as well, but the plan's scope says only plan-review paths are in scope. The same applies to the `coach_listing` header change at `coach.py:123`, which is also a shared kernel function called by both gates.
findings.location: What section / 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:75; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162; src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501
scenarios: The decision parameter is added to render_coach_notes; code_review/workflow_ops.py silently receives no decision map and emits coaching entries with decision=None — or a new required-parameter error breaks the code-review gate.
Tests for code-review coaching pass/fail depending on whether the parameter is optional-with-default — not specified in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map' with 'review_kernel.coach.render_coach_notes gains an optional `decision_map: dict[str,str] | None = None` parameter (backward-compatible); the code_review/workflow_ops.py call site requires no change since it passes no decision map.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` to the explicit change list, updating its `properties` to include `blocking` and updating the description to reflect that the op now accepts blocking findings as well as surviving ones.
evidence: plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json: properties {ticket_id, surviving} only, additionalProperties: true
Plan: 'add `blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }}` to the `coach_inputs` step's `with:`' — input schema not mentioned
Plan explicitly calls out the OUTPUT schema's additionalProperties:false closure but is silent on the input schema
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding `blocking` as a new `with:` input to `plan_review_coach_inputs` in the yaml, and updating the OUTPUT schema — but never mentions that the INPUT schema (`src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json`) only declares `{ticket_id, surviving}` and has `additionalProperties: true`. The plan's silence implicitly relies on `additionalProperties: true` to accept `blocking` without schema errors, which is a valid but undocumented assumption that contradicts the plan's explicit attention to the output schema's `additionalProperties: false` closure.
findings.location: What section / coach_inputs op change; src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json
scenarios: The input schema is used for validation or documentation generation — leaving `blocking` undeclared means the schema description stays misleading ('surviving advisory findings the coach INSTRUCTIONS are listed from') after the change.
A future schema-strict validator rejects `blocking` in the input because it is undeclared (even if additionalProperties:true currently permits it).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"blocking": { "type": "array", "description": "Blocking findings (from decide's `blocking` partition) prepended to the coachable set." }` to the input schema's properties and update the schema description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501` as a third caller of `render_coach_notes` and explicitly state whether it should receive the decision map (or confirm it is intentionally excluded because code-review doesn't use blocking-coaching yet).
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501: `coaching = review_kernel.render_coach_notes(raw_notes, applicable)`
Plan says: 'passes.render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py) gains an optional decision map … both callers (the bespoke pass4 coach in passes.py and plan_review_coach) pass it'
plan does not mention code_review/workflow_ops.py as a caller
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501 calls `review_kernel.render_coach_notes(raw_notes, applicable)` — an unenumerated caller of the shared kernel function. The plan adds an optional `decision` map parameter to `render_coach_notes`; this code-review caller is not mentioned and the plan contains no statement that it is deliberately excluded from receiving the decision tag.
findings.location: What section (render_coach_notes parameter change); src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501
scenarios: If the decision map parameter is positional or causes a signature incompatibility, code_review_coach silently breaks.
If the parameter is keyword-only with default None, the call is backward-compatible but the plan's claim that 'both callers pass it' is inaccurate — three callers exist, not two.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that the `coach_listing` header change in `review_kernel/coach.py:123` propagates to code-review via `code_review/workflow_ops.py:473`, and confirm this cross-gate change is intentional or scope the header change to plan-review only.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473: `instructions = review_kernel.coach_listing(surfaced, applicable)` — code-review calls the same function
Plan says: 'the hardcoded listing header … in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 becomes decision-neutral' without acknowledging code-review consumes it
findings.finding: Changing the header string in `review_kernel/coach.py:123` from `## Surviving advisory findings (by id)` to `## Surviving findings (by id)` also affects code-review, because `code_review/workflow_ops.py:473` calls `review_kernel.coach_listing(surfaced, applicable)` — the same shared kernel function. The plan scopes this change as plan-review-only but does not acknowledge the blast-radius to code-review prompts.
findings.location: What section (listing header change); src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 and src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473
scenarios: Code-review prompts that reference the header text by name in tests or assertions break if those assertions were comparing against '## Surviving advisory findings (by id)'.
The AC oracle `grep -niP 'advisory\s+(findings|only)' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md` passes but a parallel code-review coach prompt (if it embeds the header) may not be checked.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command to AC5, e.g. `python -m pytest tests/unit -k "subject_validator or coach" -x`, so the oracle is self-contained and unambiguous.
evidence: AC5: 'Oracle: existing subject-validator tests extended to a blocking-finding case; suite green.'
No pytest -k filter or make target named in AC5 itself
AC1 names `python -m pytest tests/unit -k "coach" -x`; AC7 names `make test`; AC5 names neither
findings.finding: AC5's oracle is stated as 'existing subject-validator tests extended to a blocking-finding case; suite green' without naming the concrete command to run. An executor must infer the command (likely `python -m pytest tests/unit -k "coach" -x` from AC1 or `make test` from AC7), making the AC5 oracle non-self-contained and potentially ambiguous about scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
scenarios: Executor is unsure whether 'suite green' means only the coach-filtered subset, the full unit suite, or `make test` — and either over- or under-validates.
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 oracle: "Oracle: existing subject-validator tests extended to a blocking-finding case; `python -m pytest tests/unit -k 'subject_validator or coach' -x` green."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the YAML `surviving:` rebinding and the op's `blocking + surfaced` derivation: either (a) keep `surviving:` carrying only advisory findings and add a new `blocking:` pass-through so the op can still do `blocking + surfaced`, OR (b) change the op to derive its trigger set from the single combined `findings` input and drop the separate `blocking` derivation — and update both the YAML and op sections consistently.
evidence: 'change `coach_notes`'s `surviving:` binding to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}`' — YAML section
'`plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:475): derive the trigger set from the union of `blocking` + `surfaced` (it already receives both)' — Op section
If `surviving:` is now the combined list and `blocking:` is still a separate wire, `blocking + surfaced` = blocking + (blocking + advisory) = double-counted blocking entries in the trigger set
findings.finding: The YAML change and the op change for `plan_review_coach` are potentially contradictory: the plan instructs changing `coach_notes`'s `surviving:` binding to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` (the combined blocking+advisory list), while simultaneously instructing the `plan_review_coach` op to "derive the trigger set from the union of `blocking + surfaced` (it already receives both)". If `surfaced`/`surviving` now carries the already-combined list AND `blocking` is still wired separately, the op's `blocking + surfaced` derivation would double-count blocking findings in the trigger set.
findings.location: What section — YAML change: 'change coach_notes's surviving: binding to ${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}'; Op change: 'derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced (it already receives both)'
scenarios: A blocking finding generates two coaching entries (one from the blocking wire, one from the combined-findings wire) when the op does `blocking + surfaced`.
The trigger set used for move selection inside the op diverges from the `findings` list used by the coach prompt, producing mismatched coaching output.
findings.suggested_fix: Option (b) is simpler: rename the `surviving:` YAML parameter to `findings:` (or keep the key but document it now carries the combined list), and update `plan_review_coach`'s internal derivation to iterate over the single `findings` input rather than `blocking + surfaced`. Remove the separate `blocking:` wire to `coach_notes` if it exists, since `findings` already contains blocking entries. Update AC-2's oracle to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design-doc entry or CLAUDE.md note (or inline schema/sidecar comment) capturing the architectural decision: coaching covers both blocking and advisory findings, `decision` tag distinguishes them, and the `plan_review_coach_inputs` `findings` output is the canonical gate condition — so future maintainers have a durable reference.
evidence: Plan describes schema closure change ('CLOSED … additionalProperties: false … add "findings"') with no corresponding doc artifact
Plan describes sidecar slim field-list addition ('add `decision` to that list') with no corresponding doc artifact

The architectural decision 'coaching now covers blocking findings' changes the observable contract of plan-review for all consumers (CLI, sidecar, downstream) but is captured only in the PR description, not in any persistent reference
Scope section lists no documentation deliverable
findings.finding: The plan introduces a meaningful architectural contract change — the closed output schema gains a new property, the sidecar slim field list gains `decision`, the workflow gate condition changes from `steps.decide.outputs.surfaced` to `steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings`, and the coaching pipeline's responsibility is formally widened to cover blocking findings — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is planned to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## What (all sub-bullets), ## Scope, src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json, src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py
scenarios: A future contributor reads the plan-review workflow and wonders why `coach_gate` fires on `findings` not `surfaced` — no ADR explains the 2025 decision to widen coaching scope
A maintainer extending the sidecar slim list is unaware that `decision` was added as an optional additive field with specific semantics
A new team member onboarding to plan-review has no design doc explaining the blocking-vs-advisory coaching pipeline contract
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short prose block to CLAUDE.md (or an equivalent docs/decisions/ entry) under plan-review: 'As of [date], Pass-4 coaching covers both blocking and advisory findings. The `plan_review_coach_inputs` op emits a `findings` output (blocking first) that drives the coach_gate and coach_notes step. Coaching entries carry a `decision` field ("block"|"advisory") preserved through sidecar persistence.' This satisfies both T7 (new contract documented) and T5e (novel architectural decision captured).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and add an AC oracle for) the `rebar review-plan` CLI behavior when a blocking finding has no coaching entry — e.g., silently omit the coaching subsection, or show a neutral 'no coaching available' note — so the no-coaching case is unambiguous.
evidence: 'OUT: any change to move-registry contents' — some blocking findings may have no registered move and thus no coaching entry
AC6 oracle only asserts the happy path: 'asserting the rendered text contains the guide_url for a blocking finding' — no oracle for the no-coaching-entry case
The plan does not specify whether the CLI should silently omit the coaching section, show a placeholder, or surface a warning when a blocking finding has no coaching entry
findings.finding: The CLI rendering path for blocking findings only specifies the happy path (blocking finding has a coaching entry → render coaching line + guide_url). The plan does not specify what `rebar review-plan` renders when a blocking finding has no coaching entry (e.g., the finding's criterion has no registered move, or the coach op emits no note for it). Since move-registry changes are explicitly OUT of scope, some blocking findings may legitimately produce no coaching entry, leaving the CLI behavior undefined.
findings.location: What section, CLI bullet: 'rebar review-plan renders the coaching line + guide link under blocking findings the same way it does for advisories'; AC6 oracle
scenarios: A blocking finding whose criterion has no move in the registry produces no coaching entry; the CLI silently shows the finding with no coaching line, which operators may misread as a rendering bug or omission.
Coach op emits partial results (notes for some blocking findings but not all); CLI shows inconsistent coaching coverage with no explanation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'additionally, assert that a blocking finding with no coaching entry renders without a coaching line and without error (graceful omission).' In the CLI section, add a sentence: 'If no coaching entry exists for a blocking finding, the finding is rendered normally with no coaching subsection.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a regression test case to AC2's `plan_review_coach_inputs` workflow test that provides blocking=[] and surfaced=[1 advisory finding], asserting the `findings` output is non-empty and the gate condition remains truthy (preserving current advisory-only behavior).
evidence: 'change coach_gate's when: to ${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }} (fires when EITHER bucket is non-empty)'
AC2 oracle only specifies '1 blocking + 1 surfaced finding' scenario — no all-advisory (blocking=[]) regression case
The old gate condition was ${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }} which would be truthy for non-empty advisories
findings.finding: No AC covers the backward-compatibility edge case where `blocking` is empty (i.e., a review with only advisory findings, the current common case). The `coach_gate` condition changes from `${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}` to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}`; if `coachable = blocking + surviving` produces an empty list when blocking=[] and surviving=[] but a non-empty list when blocking=[] and surviving=[...], the gate behavior for the all-advisory case must match old behavior. This is asserted nowhere.
findings.location: AC2 / plan-review.yaml coach_gate when: condition change
scenarios: If the `findings` output from `plan_review_coach_inputs` is computed differently from the old `surfaced` output (e.g., empty-list vs. null/undefined for zero findings), the coach gate could silently stop firing for existing advisory-only reviews.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the CLI rendering change requires per-finding interleaving of coaching under its blocking finding (a structural renderer change), or whether the existing flat post-list rendering with guide_url present satisfies the intent — the current plan description says 'under each blocking finding' but the existing renderer and AC oracle both point to a flat list approach.
evidence: Plan: 'CLI: `rebar review-plan` renders the coaching line + guide link under blocking findings the same way it does for advisories'
_llm_commands.py:536-537: prints blocking findings in a loop
_llm_commands.py:551-553: prints ALL coaching notes together after all findings — no per-finding association
AC6: 'CLI rendering unit test asserting the rendered text contains the guide_url for a blocking finding' — softer than 'under each blocking finding'
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'CLI: `rebar review-plan` renders the coaching line + guide link **under** blocking findings the same way it does for advisories' — but the current `_render_plan_review_text` implementation prints all coaching notes as a flat block *after* all findings (lines 551-553), not structurally associated with each individual finding. The existing rendering loop at line 536-537 prints blocking findings, then at 539-542 advisory findings, then at 551-553 all coaching notes together. No change to this rendering architecture is specified in the plan, yet the description implies per-finding association.
findings.location: CLI section of the plan; src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:536-553 (_render_plan_review_text)
scenarios: A developer implementing AC6 interprets 'under each blocking finding' as requiring a renderer rewrite to interleave coaching under its associated finding — but the AC oracle only requires guide_url to appear somewhere in the output, so the existing flat rendering suffices for the AC.
The discrepancy between plan prose ('under blocking findings') and current architecture (flat post-list) creates ambiguity about whether a structural renderer change is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the 'What' section explaining why widening at the `coach_inputs` seam (single LLM call, unified findings list, WS3 subject-validator path preserved for all findings) is the right structural choice over a separate parallel branch or post-assembly injection.
evidence: The 'What' section describes the changes mechanically but contains no sentence of the form 'this approach is chosen because…' or 'an alternative would be X but Y'.
The 'Why' section explains the business motivation (E6/F1 recurrence) but not the design rationale for the chosen seam.
ABSENCE: no positive rationale for widening at coach_inputs vs a separate branch or post-assembly injection.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for WHY the chosen mechanism (widening the `coach_inputs` op to emit a unified `findings` output and changing the gate condition) is the right structural approach, as opposed to plausible alternatives such as (a) a separate parallel coach branch that fires exclusively on blocking findings, or (b) post-assembly injection of coaching into blocking findings inside `plan_review_coach` without touching the gate condition. The plan's 'Why' section motivates the goal but the 'What' section only describes the changes without stating why this decomposition is architecturally correct (e.g., that centralizing at `coach_inputs` keeps a single LLM call, preserves the WS3 subject-validator path for all findings, and avoids branching explosion in the YAML).
findings.location: ## What (plan body, approach description)
scenarios: An implementer choosing between widening coach_inputs vs adding a second coach branch in the YAML has no stated reason to prefer the former — they may implement a structurally different (potentially higher blast-radius) approach that still satisfies the ACs.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, after the first paragraph, add: 'This seam is the right integration point because it keeps a single LLM coach call regardless of how many findings exist (blocking + advisory), preserves the existing WS3 subject-validator path for all notes, and avoids adding a second branch arm to the YAML — the lowest blast-radius widening consistent with the reviewer/writer separation invariant.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `plan_review_coach_inputs` should log a warning (or assert in tests) when the `findings` output is empty but `blocking` input was non-empty, and add an AC oracle covering the block-only (surfaced=0, blocking>0) case to catch silent-drop regressions.
evidence: Current op code: `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get('surviving') or [])` — silently defaults to [] if missing.

The plan specifies `blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }}` is added to the YAML with: block, but does not specify that the op should warn/log when blocking is provided but empty vs not provided at all.
AC oracle: 'synthetic verdict with 1 block + 1 advisory' — no edge case for blocking-present but wiring-absent.
findings.finding: The `coach_gate` else arm — which fires when `findings` is empty (pure PASS verdict) — already correctly passes `notes: []`. However, the plan does not specify what happens in the edge case where `blocking` is non-empty but `coach_inputs` fails to include blocking findings in the `findings` output (e.g., if the `blocking` input to `coach_inputs` is missing or empty due to a YAML wiring mistake). In that case the gate fires the else arm and blocking findings silently receive no coaching, with no error signal. The plan's YAML change adds `blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }}` to `coach_inputs`, but there is no guard or assertion in `plan_review_coach_inputs` to verify the input was received — the op's current code does `list(ctx.inputs.get('surviving') or [])` silently tolerating missing inputs, and the widened op will similarly tolerate a missing `blocking` key. The AC oracle only covers the happy path (synthetic 1 block + 1 advisory).
findings.location: ## What — `plan_review_coach_inputs` op change; plan-review.yaml coach_inputs step with: section
scenarios: If a future YAML refactor drops the `blocking:` binding from `coach_inputs` with:, the op silently treats it as empty, blocking findings get no coaching, and no log or test catches the regression.
A block-only verdict (surfaced=0, blocking>0) where the YAML wires `blocking` correctly but `coach_inputs` silently drops it would be caught by no existing test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion for sidecar entries: state that sidecar entries written with `decision` are safely ignored by a rolled-back reader (e.g., confirm the deserializer uses permissive parsing / extra-fields-ignored mode), or add a note that the field is forward-only and existing entries need not be backfilled or cleaned up.
evidence: Plan states 'additive optional field' but provides no explicit post-rollback schema state assertion.
T11 criterion: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
ABSENCE: No section of the plan addresses what a rolled-back reader does with pre-existing sidecar entries carrying the decision field.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema state of already-persisted sidecar entries after a rollback. Per T11, rollback safety requires the plan to assert the schema STATE after rollback, not merely imply reversibility. If the code is rolled back, sidecar entries already written with decision='block'/'advisory' will persist; the plan does not state whether the post-rollback reader tolerates or ignores this extra field.
findings.location: Scope section / sidecar slim change: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py build_payload (~lines 445-453), AC4
scenarios: Code is deployed, some reviews run and write decision-tagged sidecar entries, then code is rolled back. The old reader encounters sidecar entries with an unknown decision field and may fail strict deserialization, silently drop entries, or error on load.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope or AC4 section, add a one-line assertion such as: 'Post-rollback, sidecar entries already carrying `decision` are tolerated by the pre-change reader because build_payload output is loaded via [permissive dict/JSON] parsing that ignores unknown keys; no cleanup or backfill of persisted entries is required on rollback.'

findings.finding: The plan says 'the bespoke pass-4 invocation in passes.py is widened from advisory-only to blocking+advisory the same way' — but `pass4_coach` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:842` is not called anywhere in the live workflow path. The workflow exclusively uses the `plan_review_coach` workflow op (workflow_ops.py:475); `pass4_coach` is only called from unit tests. Widening `pass4_coach` does NOT widen the live path, and the plan conflates the two as if they were the same change.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:842; plan section 'What' bullet on passes.py

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
